topic: The Day Detroit Died: The Single Decision That Destroyed America's Greatest City

chosenAngle.title: The Highway That Killed Detroit: How Washington Demolished a City to Build a Road
chosenAngle.hook: The federal government spent billions building the infrastructure that was supposed to modernize Detroit — and in doing so, physically demolished the thriving Black neighborhoods that were its economic and cultural heart.
chosenAngle.description: This documentary would trace how the Interstate Highway Act of 1956 and subsequent urban renewal projects routed I-375 and I-75 directly through Black Bottom and Paradise Valley — deliberately displacing an estimated 130,000 residents and erasing Detroit's most vital commercial districts. It would argue that Detroit's decline was not accidental urban entropy but an engineered outcome of federal housing policy, racially motivated highway routing, and suburban subsidies that made leaving the city a white middle-class entitlement and staying a Black working-class trap.

script:
# DETROIT: HOW A CITY DISAPPEARS

---

**[OPEN — HOOK]**

Forty square miles of vacant land sitting inside a major American city.

Not the aftermath of a war. Not a natural disaster. Just emptiness — where buildings stood, where people raised children, where a city had a pulse. You can drive through it today. Block after block of foundations and weeds and the occasional stripped house that nobody got around to tearing down.

Someone chose this. That's the part nobody wants to say out loud.

This is how it happened.

---

**[SECTION 1 — THE PEAK]**

In 1950, Detroit was the fourth largest city in the United States. Population: 1.85 million. The undisputed capital of the world's most powerful industry — a place where a man with a high school diploma could walk off the street, take a union job at one of the Big Three automakers, and buy a house, a car, and raise a family on a single paycheck. The American Dream wasn't a metaphor here. It was a wage. A specific, negotiated, hourly wage with benefits and a pension attached to it.

Henry Ford introduced the moving assembly line at Highland Park in 1913 and remade the global economy. By the 1940s, Detroit had retooled its entire industrial base for war production — manufacturing tanks, bombers, and jeeps at a scale that helped win World War II. President Roosevelt called it the Arsenal of Democracy. He wasn't exaggerating. Detroit's factories were a material reason the Allied powers won.

Block after block of working-class neighborhoods, union halls, churches, storefronts. A city that had figured out — however imperfectly — how to absorb immigrants from Eastern Europe and migrants from the American South and turn them into a middle class.

Then: July 18, 2013. That same city filed the largest municipal bankruptcy in American history. Eighteen to twenty billion dollars in debt. Seventy-eight thousand abandoned structures. Forty percent of its streetlights dark. A median household income of twenty-six thousand dollars — roughly half the national average.

The question isn't just what happened to Detroit.

It's who made it happen. And here's what most accounts get wrong: the answer doesn't start with the auto industry. It starts with a road.

---

**[SECTION 2 — THE HIGHWAY]**

1956. President Eisenhower signs the Federal-Aid Highway Act — the largest public works project in American history. Forty-one thousand miles of interstate highway, sold to the American public as a national defense measure. Cold War infrastructure. Roads that would let the military move fast if the Soviets came. That was the story.

What the story left out: someone had to decide where the road went.

In Detroit, that decision was not neutral. The routes selected for I-375 and I-75 did not cut through empty land or industrial zones. They cut directly through Black Bottom and Paradise Valley — two of the most densely populated and economically vital Black neighborhoods in the entire Midwest. Federal engineers knew exactly what was there. They had the maps. They had the census data.

Detroit's mayor at the time was Albert Cobo (who would go on to have a major civic arena named after him, which tells you something about how this city processed its own history). He had run openly against public housing integration. He used the language of "urban renewal" and "slum clearance" — phrases that, in 1950s America, functioned as a reliable synonym for the demolition of Black communities.

That language is important. Because it obscured what was actually being erased.

---

**[SECTION 3 — WHAT WAS ACTUALLY LOST]**

Black Bottom and Paradise Valley were not blighted wastelands.

They were thriving, self-contained ecosystems — built by necessity, because Black Detroiters were legally and socially barred from living almost anywhere else in the city or its suburbs. Paradise Valley was Detroit's answer to Harlem: a dense corridor of Black-owned businesses, jazz clubs, hotels, law offices, medical practices, and churches. Ella Fitzgerald performed there. Duke Ellington performed there. The hotel district along St. Antoine Street served Black travelers who were refused rooms everywhere else in the city.

The neighborhood was economically productive, culturally irreplaceable, and held together by the kind of social bonds that don't appear on any balance sheet — which made them easy to ignore when the bulldozers arrived.

Highway construction and urban renewal programs displaced an estimated 130,000 Detroit residents. The overwhelming majority were Black. Their businesses were demolished. Their social networks were severed. The equity they had built over decades — in homes, in storefronts, in community institutions — simply vanished. They were not relocated to equivalent neighborhoods. They were pushed outward, into areas that received no comparable investment, into a housing market already being systematically strangled by federal redlining policy.

One hundred and thirty thousand people. Gone.

But the highway wasn't finished doing damage. Because once it was built, it started doing something else entirely — something more calculated than simple demolition.

---

**[PATTERN INTERRUPT #1]**

You've probably heard the term "white flight." White families leaving the city for the suburbs as Black populations moved in. It gets told as a story about individual choices — fear, prejudice, decisions made around kitchen tables in the early 1960s.

That framing is incomplete to the point of being dishonest.

White flight wasn't a spontaneous reaction. It was a subsidized program. The federal government paid for it. The same expressways that demolished Paradise Valley created fast, cheap access to new suburban developments in Dearborn, Warren, and Livonia — suburbs that were, by explicit design of their zoning laws and deed restrictions, entirely white. The Federal Housing Administration refused to guarantee mortgages in racially mixed or Black neighborhoods while actively financing the white suburbs those highways now connected to.

Here's what to track: the federal government demolished Black wealth inside the city, subsidized white departure from the city, and denied Black families the mortgages that would have let them follow. All three moves, simultaneously, by the same government.

Detroit was becoming a city that some people were paid to leave — and others were prevented from rebuilding.

That pressure had to go somewhere. In the summer of 1967, it did.

---

**[SECTION 4 — THE REBELLION]**

July 23rd. Detroit police raid an unlicensed bar on 12th Street — a neighborhood that had absorbed many of the residents displaced by the highways. What followed lasted five days.

Forty-three people killed. 1,189 injured. Over 7,200 arrested. More than 2,000 buildings destroyed.

The 1967 Detroit Rebellion grew from specific, documented grievances: a nearly all-white police force in an increasingly Black city, systematic housing discrimination, communities demolished with nothing built in their place. But its consequences fell hardest on the people whose conditions had caused it. Insurance costs in Detroit spiked overnight. Businesses that could leave, left. White residents who had been weighing the suburbs made their decision.

The population loss, which had been gradual, became a hemorrhage.

The highways had already built the exit ramps. Now everyone was using them.

---

**[SECTION 5 — THE INDUSTRY]**

Throughout the 1960s, Japanese automakers had been quietly exporting small, fuel-efficient vehicles to the American market. The Big Three — holding roughly 90 percent of U.S. car sales — looked at those cars and did nothing. Not quite nothing. They looked at them and laughed.

Then 1973. The Arab Oil Embargo.

Demand shifted in a matter of months. Ford, GM, and Chrysler had nothing to offer the suddenly fuel-conscious American consumer. Their near-total market share fell below 45 percent by 2010. The UAW, under Walter Reuther, had won genuinely historic wages and benefits for autoworkers — a real achievement, one that lifted hundreds of thousands of families into the middle class — but the resulting legacy costs made American automakers structurally uncompetitive against Japanese and German rivals who carried no equivalent obligations. Management and labor had together built companies that couldn't absorb a market shift they had chosen not to prepare for.

As the industry contracted and residents fled, Detroit's tax base collapsed. But its costs didn't. The city was still responsible for the same infrastructure, the same pension obligations, the same services — for a population now less than half the size it had been. Every year, the math got worse. Every year, the exit accelerated.

Then the federal government made one final move — and it is the sharpest irony in this entire story.

---

**[PATTERN INTERRUPT #2]**

2008. The federal government spends $49.5 billion bailing out General Motors and $12.5 billion bailing out Chrysler.

Sixty-two billion dollars. Public money.

Here's what that money did not do. Between 2000 and 2010, the Big Three collectively shed over 200,000 Detroit-area jobs — even after the bailout. The federal government saved the corporations. It did not save the workers. It did not save the city. The institution Detroit had built its entire identity around was rescued with public money and then continued downsizing the city anyway.

On July 18, 2013 — four years after the rescue — Detroit filed for Chapter 9 bankruptcy. Emergency Manager Kevyn Orr stated it plainly: *"The city of Detroit cannot and is not going to pay its debts."*

Four years. That's how long it took.

---

**[SECTION 6 — THE NUMBERS]**

A population that fell from 1.85 million to 688,000. More than sixty percent. Gone.

A school system that collapsed from 167,000 students in 2000 to 47,000 by 2015. A homicide rate of 54.6 per 100,000 in 2012 — nearly eleven times the national average. A police force so under-resourced that average response time stretched to fifty-eight minutes, against a national average of eleven.

Each of those numbers is the downstream consequence of a specific decision — a highway route chosen in the 1950s, a mortgage denied, a neighborhood demolished, a small car ignored, a city abandoned by the very institutions it had made powerful and wealthy.

None of this was inevitable. Every data point traces back to a room where someone made a choice.

Every single one.

---

**[CLOSE]**

Federal policy demolished the neighborhoods that anchored Black economic life. Those same highways subsidized white departure. The auto industry refused to adapt. The bailout rescued the companies without rescuing the community. And the city that had built the modern world was handed the bill for its own dismantling.

No single decision destroyed Detroit. What destroyed Detroit was a system that kept making the same decision — decade after decade, at every level of government and industry — that some people's neighborhoods were worth building and other people's neighborhoods were in the way.

Forty square miles of vacant land.

Somebody's signature is on every acre of it.
